---
title: "An appraisal of allergic disorders in India and an urgent call for action"
author: "Mamidipudi Thirumala Krishna, Saibal Moitra, Padukudru Anand Mahesh, Vinay Mehta, Pudupakkam Vedanthan, Devasahayam Jesudas Christopher"
year: 2020
doi: "10.1016/j.waojou.2020.100446"
journal:
   name: "World Allergy Organization Journal"
volume: "13(7):100446"
pmid: "32774662"
pmcid: "PMC7398972"
access: "Open access — CC BY-NC-ND 4.0. PDF accessed directly."
source_quality: "Full text read from PDF."
---

# An appraisal of allergic disorders in India and an urgent call for action

**Authors:** Mamidipudi Thirumala Krishna, Saibal Moitra, Padukudru Anand Mahesh, Vinay Mehta, Pudupakkam Vedanthan, Devasahayam Jesudas Christopher
**Year:** 2020
**DOI:** 10.1016/j.waojou.2020.100446
**Journal:** World Allergy Organization Journal, 13(7):100446
**PMID:** 32774662 | **PMCID:** PMC7398972

## Why this paper

Open-access paper that both documents the evidence base and explicitly names gaps, infrastructure failures, and calls for action. Uniquely valuable because it presents original aggregation — specifically Table 2, a compiled table of food allergen sensitisation studies conducted in India. This is the only paper in our set that synthesises multiple India-specific food allergen studies in one place.

## Extracted points — food allergy section and Table 2

> **Source quality note:** Full text read from PDF. All numbers below are from the paper. Text in quotation marks is taken directly. Table 2 data reproduced below with study details.

### Food allergy prevalence — key numbers

1. EuroPrevall-INCO children data (India): "EuroPrevall-INCO survey revealed food allergy to be 0.14% among children from 2 participating Indian centres." — [FULL TEXT, direct quote]
   Note: "2 participating Indian centres" = Mysore and Bengaluru (Karnataka).

2. Sensitisation paradox stated explicitly: "sensitisation rate was quite high (19.1%) to food allergens in Indian children in this survey. In other words, despite high sensitisation rates, the prevalence of clinically relevant food allergies was extremely low." — [FULL TEXT, direct quote]

3. Peanut specifically: "peanut allergy in children was found to be quite low (0.03%), although peanut sensitisation was high at 6.3%." — [FULL TEXT, direct quote]
   Context: These numbers are from the EuroPrevall-INCO children's survey.

4. EuroPrevall-INCO adult data: "The EuroPrevall-INCO study in an adult population conducted in 2 centres in Karnataka state showed a very high sensitisation rate of 26.5%, whereas rates of 'probable food allergy' were low at 1.2%." — [FULL TEXT, direct quote]

5. The paper flags: "Further research is needed to investigate why prevalence of clinically relevant food allergy is low in India despite high rates of sensitisation." — [FULL TEXT, direct quote]

### Table 2 — Food allergen sensitisation studies in India

The paper presents a compiled table (Table 2) of 9 food allergen sensitisation studies from India. Reproduced here with all data from the PDF:

| Study | Location | N | Foods tested | Key sensitisation findings |
|-------|----------|---|--------------|---------------------------|
| Patil SP et al. 2001 | Allergy clinic, Bombay (Western India) | 1400 | Chickpea and other legumes | Self-reported chickpea allergy: 59; History + SPT positive for chickpea: 41; DBPCFC positive: 31 |
| Kumari D et al. 2006 | Tertiary care hospital, New Delhi (North India) | 816 | Blackgram | Self-reported black gram: 35/816; History + SPT positive: 16/35 (12.1%); History + specific IgE: 14/35; DBPCFC: 4/14 subjects where performed |
| Kumar R et al. 2006 | Tertiary care hospital, New Delhi | 216 | 20 foods: Rice, Blackgram, Lentils, Citrus, Pea, Maize, Lima bean, French bean | Sensitisation rates: Rice 11.1%; Blackgram 10.1%; Lentils 9.7%; Citrus 9.2%; Pea 6%; Maize 6%; Lima bean 5.5%; French bean 5.5% |
| Kumar R et al. 2007 | Tertiary care hospital, New Delhi | 1200 | Rice | Self-reported rice allergy: 165/1200; History + SPT positive: 20/165 (12.1%); History + specific IgE: 13/165; DBPCFC: 6/10 subjects where performed |
| Harish Babu et al. 2008 | Community study, Mysore (South India), adults | 741 | Eggplant only | Self-reported eggplant allergy: ~9.2%; History + SPT positive: 4.3%; History + specific IgE: 0.8% |
| Misra A et al. 2008 | Hospital, Lucknow (North India) | 76 | Legumes | Sensitisation to legumes: 30; History + SPT positive: 6 |
| Mandal J et al. 2009 | Allergy unit, tertiary care hospital, Kolkata (Eastern India) | 684 | Cereals, milk, egg, fish, fruits and vegetables | Sensitisation rates: Prawn 53.5%; Brinjal (eggplant) 45.6%; Banana 40.6%; Egg 34.9%; Wheat 28.4%; Milk 20.5% |
| Kumar R et al. 2010 | Tertiary care hospital, New Delhi | 1860 | Rice, Blackgram, Lentil, Citrus, Pea, Maize, Banana | Sensitisation rates: Rice 6.2%; Blackgram 5.9%; Lentil 5.5%; Citrus 5.3%; Pea 3.8%; Maize 3.8%; Banana 3.6% |
| Dey D et al. 2014 | Clinic, Kolkata (Eastern India) | 5161 | 46 foods: Banana, Brinjal, Lentils, Wheat, Egg | Sensitisation rates: Banana 32.4%; Brinjal 29.4%; Egg 23.1%; Wheat 21.7%; Lentils 10.4% |
| Mahesh PA et al. 2016 | General population, Mysore and Bengaluru (EuroPrevall adult, N=10,931 screened, N=587 case-control) | 10931 | 24 EuroPrevall priority foods (fish, cow's milk, egg, mustard, soya, peanut, lentil, wheat, buckwheat, walnut, poppy seed, melon, sunflower, corn, banana, sesame, shrimp, tomato, kiwi, carrot, celery, apple, peach, hazelnut) | % sensitisation and probable allergy: Shrimp 15.53%/0.0%; Wheat 11.93%/0.02%; Peanut 8.73%/0.0%; Apple 7.27%/0.50%; Egg 2.56%/0.05%; Milk 2.71%/0.50%; Fish 0.5%/0.0% |
| Chogtu B et al. 2017 | University teaching hospital, Karnataka | 2219 | 31 foods: Red gram, Green gram, Red kidney beans, Wheat, Egg | Sensitisation rates: Red gram 12.6%; Green gram 12.5%; Red kidney beans 10.9%; Wheat 9.6%; Egg 6.9% |
| Gobinaath T R et al. 2018 | School children, urban and rural schools | 350 | 10 foods: Prawn, Peanut, Fish, Milk, Banana | Sensitisation rates: Prawn 17.7% urban / 5.7% rural; Peanut 19.6% urban / 10.4% rural; Fish 17.7% urban / 5.7% rural; Milk 17.7% urban / 5.2% rural; Banana 2.5% urban / 1.0% rural |
| Li J et al. 2019 | General population, Mysore and Bengaluru (EuroPrevall children, N=35,549 screened in China/India/Russia, India N=5677 children; case-control N=450) | 5677 | 25 EuroPrevall priority foods (hen's egg, cow's milk, peanut, soy, hazelnut, walnut, celery, kiwi, apple, peach, sesame, mustard, wheat, fish, shrimp, buckwheat, corn, carrot, rice, tomato, melon, banana, lentils, sunflower seeds, poppy seeds) | % sensitisation and probable allergy: Shrimp 10.3%/0.0%; Wheat 6.7%/0.0%; Peanut 6.3%/0.0%; Apple 4.2%/0.0%; Milk 2.1%/0.0%; Egg 1.7%/0.05%; Fish 0.4%/0.0% |

> Note: "Probable allergy" in the EuroPrevall studies requires reported symptoms within 2 hours of consumption AND either positive specific IgE or positive SPT. This is not the same as confirmed allergy via oral food challenge (OFC).

### Key observations from Table 2

- **Consistent across multiple studies**: black gram, rice, lentil, chickpea, prawn, eggplant (brinjal), banana, egg, and wheat appear repeatedly.
- **Geographic variation is real**: Kolkata study (Mandal 2009) shows very high prawn sensitisation (53.5%); this reflects East India's fish/prawn diet. Delhi studies concentrate on legumes. Karnataka studies concentrate on EuroPrevall panel.
- **Urban-rural gap confirmed** (Gobinaath 2018): prawn, peanut, fish, and milk sensitisation all significantly higher in urban children than rural in the same region.
- **Large clinic-based studies**: Most studies are from allergy clinic or hospital populations, not general population. Sensitisation figures from these settings are expected to be higher than population-level prevalence.

## Infrastructure and policy gaps documented

6. "High quality allergen extracts for skin tests and adrenaline auto-injectors are currently not available in India." — [FULL TEXT, direct quote from abstract]
   For us: Without standardised allergen extracts, SPT-based diagnosis is unreliable — this is a structural reason why Indian prevalence data may be underestimated or inconsistent.

7. "Higher postgraduate specialist training programmes in Allergy and Immunology are also not available." — [FULL TEXT, direct quote from abstract]

8. Evidence from high-income Western countries is "not directly applicable to India due to important confounders such as ethnicity, air pollution, high rates of parasitic infestation, and other infections." — [FULL TEXT, direct quote from abstract]
   For us: Explicit statement from a multi-centre Indian authorship team that Western allergen lists should not be directly imported to India.

## What this paper does not address

- FSSAI allergen labelling requirements: DON'T KNOW — not covered in the food allergy section
- Mechanism of the sensitisation-reactivity paradox: flagged as a gap, not explained
- North-East India food allergen data: not represented in Table 2
